facts.text: Pottersville was originally called Potters Mills, renamed when a post office opened and a Potter family member became the first postmaster.
The Lamington River powered a diverse array of mills—flour, saw, foundry, woolen, tannery, grist, cotton, basket—making the village a 19th‑century industrial hub.
In 1888 the Rockaway Valley Railroad built a spur to Pottersville to ship peaches, but the crop’s failure ended the line by 1912.
Pottersville’s historic district, listed in 1990, preserves 44 buildings from the 1850s to 1920s, showcasing the village’s rural character.
The village sits at the confluence of Hunterdon, Somerset, and Morris counties along the Lamington River, covering about 0.9 square miles.
The Purnell School, a private all‑girls boarding high school founded in 1963, once operated in Pottersville before closing in 2021.
